Get involved

Join the Lab

We hire for curiosity and rigor, not a checklist. If figuring out how birds fly — and what that means for engineering — sounds like your kind of problem, read on.

The short version

We study birds to find the principles behind flight, then test which of those principles actually earn a place in engineered systems. You might measure a real raptor in flight at our CALI flight hall and model its aerodynamics the same week. You do not need training in both biology and engineering — most people arrive strong in one and pick up the other here.

What to expect

  • Your own project, from question to publication, with weekly mentoring — not a task list.
  • A broad toolkit: wind tunnels, motion capture, CFD, flight-dynamics modeling, machine learning, and live-animal work.
  • Real output: conference travel, co-authored papers, and open data and code you can point to.
  • A lab that writes down how it works — see the public Lab Guide — and takes safety, inclusion, and honest science seriously.

Openings right now

Undergraduates (UC Davis) — We interview one or two students most quarters; all majors welcome. Strong researchers can be paid after a trial quarter. Fastest way in: the interest form.

Graduate students — No funded openings at the moment, but if you can bring or pursue a fellowship, get in touch. Christina advises in both Mechanical & Aerospace Engineering (MS/PhD) and Animal Behavior (PhD). Interviews start in early fall, ahead of UC Davis’s December deadlines — reach out then to be considered for the lab specifically.

Postdocs — No funded openings now; if you’re interested, email and we can look at fellowship options together.

How to reach out

  • UC Davis undergrads → the interest form.
  • Everyone else → email Christina (harvey@ucdavis.edu) with a CV, transcript, and a few lines on what you’d like to work on.

A good first email is four or five lines: who you are, why this lab (name a project or paper that caught you), what you want to learn, and your timing. Attach a CV and transcript. She aims to reply within two weeks — nudge her if she doesn’t. Address it to “Dr. Harvey” to start; first names once you’re in.

New to this? Two useful reads: applying to academic positions and how to email a professor about research.

Bio-Informed Research & Design Lab

Department of Mechanical & Aerospace Engineering, UC Davis, Davis, CA
